name: Lake George
description:
Known as the "Queen of American Lakes," Lake George is a crown jewel of the Adirondack region. Stretching 32 miles through the southeastern Adirondacks, this crystal-clear lake is renowned for its exceptional water clarity and stunning mountain scenery. The lake's depth reaches 196 feet, and its 109 miles of shoreline are dotted with over 170 islands, many of which are available for primitive camping.

Lake George offers outstanding fishing opportunities year-round, with lake trout and landlocked salmon thriving in its deep, cold waters. Smallmouth and largemouth bass provide excellent action along rocky shorelines and submerged structure, while northern pike lurk in the weedy bays. Yellow perch and brown trout round out a diverse fishery that draws anglers from across the Northeast.

The surrounding communities of Lake George Village, Bolton Landing, Hague, and Ticonderoga provide full services for visiting anglers, including marinas, bait shops, and boat launches. The lake's clear waters also make it a premier destination for recreational boating, swimming, and scuba diving, ensuring that a fishing trip to Lake George is a complete outdoor experience.

name: Oneida Lake
description:
Oneida Lake holds the distinction of being the largest lake located entirely within New York State. Spanning over 51,000 acres across four counties, this shallow, fertile body of water is widely regarded as one of the premier walleye fisheries in the northeastern United States. Its relatively uniform depth and expansive shoals create ideal habitat for forage fish, which in turn support a thriving population of predator species.

The walleye fishing on Oneida Lake is legendary, with spring and fall runs producing fish in the 3- to 8-pound range and occasional trophies exceeding 10 pounds. Yellow perch are extraordinarily abundant and provide fast action for families and ice anglers alike. Smallmouth bass fishing is equally impressive, with rocky reefs and gravel bars holding large concentrations of hard-fighting bronzebacks throughout the summer months.

Four counties border Oneida Lake, and the communities of Sylvan Beach, Brewerton, Constantia, and Bridgeport offer excellent access with well-maintained public launches and full-service marinas. The lake's relatively shallow depth of 55 feet makes it well-suited to a variety of fishing techniques, from trolling and jigging to drift fishing and casting. Tiger musky and channel catfish add bonus opportunities for anglers seeking something different.

name: Seneca Lake
description:
Seneca Lake is the deepest of New York's famous Finger Lakes, plunging to an extraordinary 618 feet. With over 43,000 acres of surface area, it is also the second largest Finger Lake and one of the most productive fisheries in the state. The lake's immense depth creates a cold-water environment that supports excellent populations of lake trout, rainbow trout, brown trout, and landlocked Atlantic salmon.

Trolling is the primary method for targeting the cold-water species that Seneca Lake is famous for. Lake trout are found throughout the water column depending on the season, and fish in the 5- to 15-pound class are common. Rainbow trout and landlocked salmon provide thrilling surface action during spring and fall when water temperatures bring them into the upper layers. Smallmouth bass inhabit the rocky shorelines and offer outstanding catch-and-release fishing from late spring through early fall.

The Finger Lakes wine region surrounds Seneca Lake, with the charming towns of Geneva at the north end and Watkins Glen at the south providing excellent amenities for visiting anglers. Watkins Glen is also home to the famous gorge and state park, making the area a premier destination for combining world-class fishing with wine tasting and scenic tourism.

name: Cayuga Lake
description:
Cayuga Lake is the longest of the Finger Lakes, stretching nearly 40 miles from Ithaca in the south to Seneca Falls in the north. At 435 feet deep and nearly 43,000 acres in surface area, it ranks as one of New York's most significant fisheries. The lake's combination of deep cold water and extensive shallow flats creates diverse habitat that supports both cold-water and warm-water species in impressive numbers.

The cold-water fishery features excellent populations of lake trout, rainbow trout, brown trout, and landlocked salmon. Trollers work the deep waters with downriggers and lead-core line, regularly connecting with lake trout in the 5- to 20-pound range. The lake's tributaries, including Cayuga Inlet and Taughannock Creek, draw spawning runs of rainbow trout and salmon that provide exciting stream fishing opportunities. Smallmouth and largemouth bass thrive along the lake's rocky points, weed beds, and docks.

Home to Cornell University and the vibrant city of Ithaca, Cayuga Lake is surrounded by gorges, waterfalls, and state parks that make it one of the most scenic fishing destinations in the Northeast. The towns of Aurora, Union Springs, and Seneca Falls along the eastern and northern shores offer additional access points, marinas, and services for anglers exploring this magnificent body of water.

name: Lake Champlain
description:
Lake Champlain is the sixth largest natural freshwater lake in the United States, spanning 271,000 acres along the border of New York and Vermont. This massive waterway stretches 120 miles from Whitehall in the south to the Canadian border, with 587 miles of shoreline and depths reaching 400 feet. The lake's extraordinary size and diverse structure support one of the most varied fisheries in the Northeast, with over 80 species of fish inhabiting its waters.

Bass fishing on Lake Champlain is world-renowned, regularly hosting major tournament circuits including the Bassmaster Elite Series. Smallmouth bass in the 3- to 6-pound class inhabit the rocky reefs and ledges, while largemouth bass thrive in the weedy bays and backwaters. The lake's walleye population has surged in recent years, creating a destination fishery for this prized species. Landlocked salmon and lake trout patrol the deep main-lake basins, northern pike haunt the marshy shallows, and musky lurk in select bays and river mouths.

On the New York side, the Adirondack Mountains provide a dramatic backdrop to fishing on Champlain. Plattsburgh, the largest city on the lake's western shore, offers full services and multiple launch sites, while smaller communities like Westport, Port Henry, and Ticonderoga provide more rustic access. The lake's rich history, including Revolutionary War and War of 1812 battle sites, adds cultural depth to any fishing excursion.

name: Saranac Lake
description:
Saranac Lake sits at the heart of the Adirondack Mountains, encompassing the Upper, Middle, and Lower Saranac Lakes, which together form a connected chain of nearly 4,700 acres of pristine Adirondack water. Surrounded by old-growth forests and rugged mountain peaks, this chain of lakes offers a quintessential wilderness fishing experience with excellent access from the village of Saranac Lake.

The Saranac Lakes support a healthy cold-water fishery featuring lake trout and landlocked Atlantic salmon in the deeper sections, along with a robust warm-water fishery of smallmouth bass and northern pike in the shallower bays and around the numerous islands. Walleye have been successfully introduced and provide an additional target species, while yellow perch offer reliable action for anglers of all skill levels. The interconnected nature of the three lakes allows for extensive exploration by boat.

The village of Saranac Lake is a charming Adirondack community with a rich history as a health resort and outdoor recreation hub. Nearby Lake Placid, site of the 1932 and 1980 Winter Olympics, adds world-class amenities to the area. The region is especially popular during summer and fall, when the combination of exceptional fishing, stunning foliage, and wilderness camping on the lake's many islands creates an unforgettable outdoor experience.

name: Great Sacandaga Lake
description:
Great Sacandaga Lake is one of New York's largest bodies of water, covering 29,000 acres along the southern edge of the Adirondack Park. Originally created in 1930 as a flood-control reservoir by damming the Sacandaga River, the lake has evolved into one of the state's most popular multi-species fisheries. Its 125 miles of shoreline wind through rolling Adirondack foothills, creating an abundance of coves, points, and bays that hold fish throughout the season.

Walleye are the marquee species on Great Sacandaga, with spring and fall providing the best action as fish move to shallow structure. Northern pike grow to impressive sizes in the lake's weedy bays, and both smallmouth and largemouth bass offer outstanding fishing along rocky shorelines and submerged timber. Tiger musky, stocked by the DEC, add a trophy element to the fishery, with fish exceeding 40 inches caught each year. Brown trout are found near cold-water tributaries and in deeper sections of the lake.

The communities of Northville, Edinburg, and Broadalbin surround the lake and provide convenient access through numerous public launches and private marinas. Great Sacandaga's location at the Adirondack fringe makes it easily accessible from the Capital District and Saratoga Springs, drawing a strong contingent of weekend anglers. Ice fishing is extremely popular here, with walleye, perch, and pike providing excellent winter action.

name: Chautauqua Lake
description:
Chautauqua Lake is western New York's premier fishing destination, celebrated above all for its exceptional muskellunge fishery. This 13,156-acre lake in Chautauqua County has produced numerous trophy musky over 50 inches, earning it a reputation as one of the finest musky waters in the Northeast. The lake is divided into two distinct basins: the deeper, clearer north basin and the shallower, weedier south basin, each offering different fishing opportunities.

Musky anglers from across the country travel to Chautauqua Lake to pursue these apex predators, which benefit from the lake's abundant forage base and ideal habitat. Beyond musky, the lake supports excellent walleye fishing, particularly during spring and fall transitions. Largemouth bass thrive in the heavily vegetated south basin, while smallmouth bass prefer the rocky structure of the north basin. Yellow perch and crappie provide outstanding panfish action, especially during winter ice fishing season.

The Chautauqua Lake region is steeped in culture, with the world-famous Chautauqua Institution on its shores attracting visitors for arts, education, and recreation. The towns of Jamestown, birthplace of Lucille Ball, Mayville, and Bemus Point offer charming lakeside amenities, restaurants, and accommodations. Multiple public launches provide convenient access, and a strong network of local guides specializes in helping anglers connect with trophy musky.

name: Canandaigua Lake
description:
Canandaigua Lake is one of the most beautiful of New York's Finger Lakes, stretching 15.5 miles through the rolling hills of Ontario and Yates counties. With 10,553 acres of clear water reaching depths of 276 feet, the lake provides excellent habitat for both cold-water and warm-water fish species. The name Canandaigua comes from the Seneca word meaning "the chosen spot," and anglers who fish these waters quickly understand why.

Lake trout are the primary cold-water target, with fish commonly caught in the 3- to 10-pound range using downrigger trolling techniques in the lake's deep central basin. Rainbow trout add variety to the cold-water fishery and are particularly active during spring and fall. Smallmouth bass fishing is exceptional along the lake's rocky eastern shore, while largemouth bass inhabit the weedier southern end near Naples. Yellow perch and northern pike round out the fishery and provide consistent action throughout the season.

The city of Canandaigua at the lake's north end is a vibrant community with excellent restaurants, shops, and the historic Finger Lakes Performing Arts Center. The village of Naples at the south end is famous for its grape festival and serves as a gateway to the surrounding wine country. Public launches at both ends of the lake ensure convenient access for visiting anglers.

name: Keuka Lake
description:
Keuka Lake, affectionately known as the "Crooked Lake," is the only Y-shaped lake among New York's Finger Lakes. This distinctive shape creates 62 miles of shoreline around 11,584 acres of water, with two branches meeting at a bluff near the village of Branchport. The unique configuration provides an extraordinary variety of fishing structure and habitat, from deep cold-water basins to sheltered warm-water coves.

The cold-water fishery on Keuka Lake is outstanding, featuring healthy populations of lake trout, rainbow trout, landlocked Atlantic salmon, and brown trout. Lake trout are the most abundant cold-water species, regularly caught in the 4- to 12-pound range. Landlocked salmon, stocked by the DEC, provide acrobatic fights and are a favorite target of spring trollers. Smallmouth bass fishing is superb along the lake's rocky points and steep drop-offs, with bronze-colored warriors commonly exceeding 3 pounds.

The charming villages of Penn Yan and Hammondsport anchor the north and south ends of the lake, respectively, offering full services and authentic Finger Lakes hospitality. Hammondsport, known as the birthplace of American aviation through Glenn Curtiss's pioneering flights, sits in a stunning valley at the lake's southern tip. The surrounding vineyards and wineries make Keuka Lake a complete destination for anglers who appreciate fine wine and exceptional scenery alongside their fishing.

name: Skaneateles Lake
description:
Skaneateles Lake is widely recognized as one of the cleanest lakes in the United States, with water so pure that the city of Syracuse draws its unfiltered drinking water directly from the lake. Spanning 8,612 acres with depths reaching 315 feet, this jewel of the Finger Lakes region offers crystal-clear visibility that can exceed 30 feet on calm days. The exceptional water quality creates a pristine environment for both cold-water and warm-water fish species.

Lake trout are the premier target species, thriving in the deep, cold, well-oxygenated waters that define Skaneateles Lake. Fish in the 5- to 15-pound range are regularly caught by trollers working the thermocline during summer months. Rainbow trout provide additional cold-water action, particularly during spring and fall when they feed aggressively near the surface. Smallmouth bass fishing is excellent along the rocky shorelines and submerged ledges, while largemouth bass and yellow perch inhabit the shallower areas.

The village of Skaneateles at the lake's north end is one of the most picturesque communities in central New York, featuring elegant Victorian architecture, upscale shops, and fine dining. The lake's protected status as a water supply limits development along its shores, preserving the wild, undisturbed character that makes fishing here feel like stepping back in time. Public access is available at the state launch site, though the lake sees less fishing pressure than its neighboring Finger Lakes.

name: Lake Placid
description:
Lake Placid is a stunning 2,174-acre body of water nestled in the heart of the Adirondack High Peaks region. Despite sharing its name with the famous Olympic village on its shores, the lake itself is a serene fishing destination surrounded by towering Adirondack peaks, including Whiteface Mountain. The lake reaches depths of 150 feet and features remarkably clear water, making it both a scenic wonder and a productive fishery.

Lake trout and landlocked Atlantic salmon are the primary cold-water targets, benefiting from the lake's depth and excellent water quality. Trolling with downriggers is the most effective technique for these species, especially during the summer months when fish retreat to deeper, cooler water. Smallmouth bass provide exciting action along the rocky shorelines and around the lake's several islands, while northern pike and yellow perch inhabit the weedy bays and shallower areas.

The village of Lake Placid, host of the 1932 and 1980 Winter Olympic Games, offers world-class amenities including hotels, restaurants, and outfitters. The combination of Olympic history, Adirondack wilderness, and quality fishing makes Lake Placid a unique destination. Anglers can fish in the morning, hike to a High Peak in the afternoon, and enjoy fine dining in the evening, all within minutes of the lake's shores.

name: Schroon Lake
description:
Schroon Lake is a 4,200-acre Adirondack gem located along the I-87 corridor between Lake George and the High Peaks region. With depths reaching 152 feet and 18 miles of forested shoreline, the lake provides a classic Adirondack fishing experience with convenient accessibility. The surrounding mountains and dense forests create a picturesque setting that enhances every outing on the water.

The lake supports a balanced fishery featuring both cold-water and warm-water species. Lake trout and landlocked salmon inhabit the deep central basin, providing quality trolling opportunities from spring through fall. Smallmouth bass are plentiful along the rocky points and gravel bars, while largemouth bass hold in the weedy coves and along docks. Yellow perch and bullhead catfish offer excellent opportunities for family fishing and shore anglers.

The hamlet of Schroon Lake on the lake's western shore is a quintessential Adirondack community with a friendly atmosphere and essential angler services. The lake's location along the Northway (I-87) makes it one of the most accessible Adirondack waters, drawing anglers from the Capital District and beyond. Summer visitors enjoy the combination of fishing, swimming, and hiking, while ice fishing enthusiasts descend on the lake each winter for lake trout and perch through the ice.

name: Tupper Lake
description:
Tupper Lake is a 4,400-acre body of water in the western Adirondacks, surrounded by vast stretches of wilderness that give it a remote, untamed character. The lake reaches depths of 100 feet and features a mix of rocky shorelines, sandy beaches, and marshy bays that support a diverse array of fish species. As home to the Wild Center, a renowned natural history museum, the Tupper Lake area celebrates its deep connection to the Adirondack wild.

Lake trout are the signature cold-water species, holding in the deeper sections of the lake and providing excellent trolling action. Walleye have become an increasingly popular target, with the DEC stocking program building a strong fishable population. Smallmouth bass offer exciting catch-and-release fishing along rocky structure, while northern pike patrol the weedy shallows and can reach impressive sizes. Yellow perch and bullhead round out the fishery and provide reliable action for shore anglers and families.

The village of Tupper Lake serves as a gateway to some of the most remote wilderness in the Adirondack Park, including the Five Ponds Wilderness Area. The community offers essential services, lodging, and dining, while maintaining the authentic Adirondack character that draws visitors seeking a genuine wilderness experience. The Wild Center's exhibits and trails provide rainy-day entertainment and deepen appreciation for the natural world that makes fishing in the Adirondacks so special.

name: Cranberry Lake
description:
Cranberry Lake is a remote Adirondack treasure spanning 6,930 acres in St. Lawrence County, making it the third largest lake in the Adirondack Park. What makes Cranberry Lake truly special is its wildly irregular shoreline of 55 miles and 46 islands, creating an extraordinary amount of structure and habitat for fish. Much of the shoreline is bordered by the Cranberry Lake Wild Forest, ensuring a wilderness fishing experience that few lakes in the Northeast can match.

The warm-water fishery at Cranberry Lake is outstanding, with largemouth and smallmouth bass thriving in the lake's abundant structure of fallen timber, rocky points, and weed beds. Northern pike grow to impressive sizes in the marshy bays and backwaters, and walleye provide excellent fishing opportunities, particularly during low-light conditions. Yellow perch and bullhead catfish are plentiful and provide reliable action for anglers seeking a relaxing day on the water.

The tiny hamlet of Cranberry Lake and the nearby community of Wanakena offer basic services in a setting that feels far removed from civilization. The lake's remote location in the northwestern Adirondacks means lighter fishing pressure than more accessible waters, resulting in a higher quality experience for those willing to make the drive. Camping on the lake's islands and exploring its countless coves and channels by canoe or small boat is a time-honored Adirondack tradition.

name: Lake Ontario
description:
Lake Ontario is the smallest of the Great Lakes by surface area but one of the most productive fisheries in North America. New York's 300-mile shoreline along its southern and eastern edges provides access to world-class fishing for salmon, trout, and a host of other species. The lake's maximum depth of 802 feet and massive volume of cold, well-oxygenated water create ideal conditions for the salmonid species that have made it famous.

The salmon and trout fishing on Lake Ontario is nothing short of spectacular. Chinook salmon, commonly called king salmon, run 15 to 30 pounds with fish over 40 pounds caught each season. Coho salmon, steelhead rainbow trout, and brown trout add diversity to the catch, with each species peaking at different times from spring through fall. The tributaries that feed the lake, including the Salmon River and Sandy Creek, draw massive runs of spawning fish that create legendary stream fishing opportunities. Smallmouth bass and walleye provide outstanding warm-water fishing in the bays and along the shoreline.

Key fishing ports are distributed along the entire New York shoreline, each offering unique advantages. Oswego and the Salmon River area are ground zero for the fall salmon run. Henderson Harbor and the eastern basin produce trophy walleye and bass. Rochester and Sodus Point are premier launch points for summer trolling. The ports of Olcott and Wilson in the west provide access to productive brown trout and salmon waters near the Niagara River. Charter fishing operations run from every major port, making this world-class fishery accessible to anglers of all experience levels.

name: Lake Erie (NY)
description:
Lake Erie's New York shoreline stretches 75 miles along the state's western edge, providing access to one of the most productive walleye fisheries in the world. While New York holds only a fraction of Lake Erie's total shoreline, its waters are among the most fertile, with the shallow eastern basin supporting enormous populations of walleye, perch, and bass. The lake's maximum depth of 210 feet in this region and abundant forage create ideal conditions for trophy-caliber fish.

Walleye fishing is the primary draw, with spring and fall producing outstanding catches of fish in the 3- to 10-pound range. The evening and night bite during summer can be exceptional, with walleye feeding aggressively on schools of emerald shiners and other baitfish. Smallmouth bass fishing has exploded in recent years, with the clear eastern basin producing some of the largest bronzebacks in the Great Lakes. Yellow perch provide family-friendly action and are prized for their delicious fillets. Steelhead trout run the tributaries, including Cattaraugus Creek and other streams, from fall through spring.

Buffalo, New York's second largest city, anchors the eastern end of the lake and offers excellent urban fishing opportunities along its waterfront. The smaller communities of Dunkirk, Barcelona, and Hamburg provide additional access points and a more relaxed atmosphere. Barcelona Harbor is particularly popular with walleye anglers, while Dunkirk Harbor offers easy access to productive waters along the Chautauqua County shoreline.

name: Hudson River
description:
The Hudson River is one of America's most historic and ecologically significant waterways, flowing 315 miles from the Adirondack Mountains to New York Harbor. As a tidal estuary, the river experiences ocean tides as far north as Troy, creating a dynamic environment where freshwater and saltwater species intermingle. This unique characteristic makes the Hudson one of the most diverse fisheries in the northeastern United States, spanning ten counties along its lower and middle reaches.

Striped bass are the Hudson River's most celebrated game fish, with the river serving as a critical spawning ground for the Atlantic coast population. Fish ranging from schoolie-sized stripers to 40-pound cows can be caught from spring through fall, with peak action occurring during the spring spawning run. The annual American shad migration brings millions of fish upriver each spring, creating a beloved tradition among Hudson Valley anglers. Largemouth and smallmouth bass inhabit the river's backwaters, coves, and tributary mouths, while channel catfish and carp provide excellent bottom-fishing opportunities.

The Hudson Valley is one of New York's most scenic regions, with the Catskill Mountains to the west, historic estates along both banks, and charming river towns offering excellent access and amenities. Major cities like Albany, Poughkeepsie, Kingston, and Newburgh provide multiple launch points and urban fishing opportunities. The river's remarkable environmental recovery over the past half-century has restored it as a world-class fishery, proving that conservation and recreation can go hand in hand.

name: Long Island Sound
description:
Long Island Sound is a major saltwater estuary stretching along New York's southern coastline, separating Long Island from the Connecticut mainland. With 200 miles of New York shoreline across Westchester, Nassau, and Suffolk counties, the Sound provides metropolitan-area anglers with access to outstanding saltwater fishing within sight of the New York City skyline. Depths reaching 230 feet and strong tidal currents create a nutrient-rich environment that supports an incredible variety of game fish.

Striped bass are the marquee species, with fish migrating through the Sound from spring through fall in enormous numbers. Bluefish provide explosive surface action during summer, often corralling baitfish into frenzied schools visible from shore. Fluke, or summer flounder, are a favorite bottom-fishing target, with the Sound's sandy substrate and structure holding quality fish through the warm months. Blackfish and porgy provide excellent fall and winter fishing around rocky reefs and wrecks, while weakfish and false albacore offer thrilling light-tackle opportunities during their seasonal appearances.

The New York shoreline of Long Island Sound is dotted with harbors, marinas, and launch facilities that make this world-class fishery remarkably accessible. Port Jefferson and Northport on Long Island's north shore are major departure points for charter and private boats, while Westchester communities like Mamaroneck and New Rochelle provide quick access from the New York City metropolitan area. Shore fishing opportunities abound at parks, jetties, and beaches along the entire coastline, making the Sound one of the most democratic fisheries in the region.

name: St. Lawrence River
description:
The St. Lawrence River is one of North America's legendary fishing destinations, flowing from Lake Ontario through the famous Thousand Islands region and along New York's northern border with Canada. With 150 miles of New York shoreline, depths reaching 250 feet, and over 1,800 islands creating an impossibly complex maze of channels, shoals, and current breaks, the river offers some of the most diverse and productive fishing in the eastern United States.

Muskellunge fishing on the St. Lawrence River is world-renowned, with the Thousand Islands region producing fish over 50 inches with remarkable consistency. The river's crystal-clear water, abundant forage, and complex structure create ideal conditions for growing trophy-class musky. Smallmouth bass fishing is equally legendary, with the St. Lawrence regularly hosting major bass tournaments and producing catches that rival any water in the country. Largemouth bass, northern pike, walleye, and yellow perch add variety, ensuring that anglers can find quality fishing regardless of their preferred species.

The Thousand Islands communities of Clayton and Alexandria Bay are the heart of the St. Lawrence fishing scene, offering world-class guides, marinas, and accommodations. Cape Vincent, where Lake Ontario meets the St. Lawrence, provides access to both fisheries. Downstream, Ogdensburg and Massena offer excellent fishing along the river's broader, more powerful stretches. The region's rich maritime heritage, including the Antique Boat Museum in Clayton and Boldt Castle near Alexandria Bay, adds cultural dimension to any fishing trip on this magnificent river.
